---
title: "Architecture: how webterminal works"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Architecture: how webterminal works}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  eval = FALSE
)
```

## The problem

RStudio Server runs in a browser. Its built-in terminal pane routes
keystrokes through the IDE's RPC subsystem, which adds latency — the
terminal feels sluggish compared to a native SSH session.

The ideal solution is a standalone web terminal (ttyd or shellinabox)
running on the same host, but RStudio Server typically occupies the
only inbound port. You cannot open additional ports without changing
firewall rules or load balancer configuration.

## The solution: RStudio Server's localhost proxy

Open-source RStudio Server (not just Posit Workbench) includes a
built-in reverse proxy for localhost ports. When the IDE calls
`rstudioapi::viewer("http://127.0.0.1:7681/")`, the server:

1. Reads the per-user **port token cookie** (`kPortTokenCookie`).
2. Computes a scrambled hex ID from the token and port number via
   `transformPort(portToken, port)`.
3. Returns a URL of the form `/p/<8-or-9-hex-digits>/`.
4. Proxies all subsequent HTTP and **WebSocket Upgrade** requests on
   that path to `127.0.0.1:<port>` on the server host.

This is the same mechanism that serves Shiny apps and Plumber APIs
in the Viewer pane.

### The key config switch

In `/etc/rstudio/rserver.conf`:

```
www-proxy-localhost=1
```

This is **on by default**. If your terminal opens as a blank page,
verify this setting has not been overridden to `0`.

### URL pattern

| Edition | URL form |
|---|---|
| Open-source RStudio Server | `https://host/p/<hex>/` |
| Posit Workbench (multi-session) | `https://host/s/<session-id>/p/<hex>/` |

The `<hex>` is **not** the literal port number. You cannot guess it;
it must be computed by `rstudioapi::viewer()` or
`rstudioapi::translateLocalUrl()`.

## Request flow

```
Browser tab
  │
  ▼
ALB / reverse proxy (existing infrastructure)
  │
  ▼
rserver (port 8787)
  │  parses /p/<hex>/, validates auth cookie + port token
  │  forwards via Unix socket to rsession
  ▼
rsession
  │  proxies to 127.0.0.1:<port>
  │  handles HTTP Upgrade for WebSocket
  ▼
ttyd (port <port>, bound to 127.0.0.1)
  │  xterm.js ↔ pty via WebSocket
  ▼
bash / tmux session
```

Every hop is either TCP loopback or a Unix-domain socket. There is no
HTTP long-polling or RPC multiplexing in this path — it is a direct
WebSocket stream from xterm.js in the browser to the pty on the
server.

## Authentication and security

The `/p/<hex>/` proxy inherits RStudio Server's authentication. A
request to `/p/<hex>/` without a valid `rstudio-csrf` session cookie
is redirected to the login page.

The port-token mechanism adds a second layer: even with a valid
session cookie, a client cannot reach an arbitrary port without
knowing the per-user port token. The token is set as a cookie by the
IDE when `rstudioapi::viewer()` is called.

### Security implications

- **ttyd must bind to `127.0.0.1`**, not `0.0.0.0`. This ensures the
  terminal is only reachable through the RStudio proxy, never
  directly from the network.

- **No additional authentication is needed on ttyd itself.** Do not
  use ttyd's `--credential` flag; the proxy already gates access. A
  `--credential` would send basic-auth headers through the proxy and
  into browser history.

- **`--check-origin`** should remain enabled on ttyd. It rejects
  WebSocket handshakes whose `Origin` header doesn't match the
  request's `Host`, which prevents cross-site WebSocket hijacking.

- **Any user who can log into RStudio as your user can reach the
  terminal.** The terminal is not an additional attack surface beyond
  RStudio itself.

### Cross-user isolation

RStudio Server forwards `/p/<hex>/` requests through per-user
Unix-domain sockets with UID validation. User A cannot reach User B's
proxied ports, even if they guess the hex token. This is enforced in
`ServerSessionProxy.cpp` (AGPL source).

## WebSocket vs AJAX transport

| Backend | Transport | Latency | Copy/paste |
|---|---|---|---|
| ttyd | WebSocket (libwebsockets + libuv) | Lowest — single WS frame stream | Native browser copy/paste in xterm.js |
| shellinabox | AJAX long-polling | Higher — request/response per keystroke batch | shellinabox's own selection model |

ttyd's WebSocket transport is materially faster than RStudio Server's
built-in terminal pane, which routes through the IDE's RPC layer
(itself a WebSocket/HTTP-fallback multiplex).

## Session persistence with tmux

The `ttyd-tmux` backend wraps ttyd around a tmux session:

```
ttyd → bash -c tmux-web-attach.sh → tmux new-session -s web
                                     (or tmux attach-session -s web)
```

When you close the browser tab, the ttyd client disconnects but the
tmux server and its child processes keep running. Reopening the URL
(or calling `web_terminal("ttyd-tmux")` again) reattaches to the same
tmux session with full scrollback.

This survives:

- Browser tab close / reopen
- R session restart
- `rstudio-server` service restart (the tmux process is not a child
  of rserver)
- Network disconnections (ttyd's xterm.js client auto-reconnects)

It does **not** survive:

- Host reboot (tmux is not a system service by default)
- Explicit `terminal_stop("ttyd-tmux")` (sends SIGTERM to ttyd; tmux
  session persists but is no longer web-accessible until restarted)
- `tmux kill-server` (kills all tmux sessions)

## Daemon lifecycle

webterminal daemons are **out-of-process detached children**. When
`terminal_start()` calls `system2("bash", script, wait = FALSE)`, the
bash process forks ttyd, which immediately backgrounds itself (via
`nohup ... &` in the script). The R process is not the parent of the
daemon; the daemon is reparented to PID 1 (init).

This means:

- The daemon **survives** R session exit, `q()`, and `rstudio-server`
  restart.
- The daemon must be **explicitly stopped** with `terminal_stop()` or
  by killing the PID.
- The daemon's PID is recorded in
  `tools::R_user_dir("webterminal", "cache")/daemons.rds` so it can
  be found across R sessions.

### Port allocation

Each user gets a deterministic base port:

```
base = 4200 + (sum of UTF-8 codepoints in $USER) mod 90
```

Each backend adds a fixed offset:

| Backend | Offset | Example port (user "analyst") |
|---|---|---|
| ttyd | 0 | 4265 |
| ttyd-tmux | 1 | 4266 |
| shellinabox | 2 | 4267 |

This ensures different users on a shared host and different backends
for the same user never collide, without requiring a port broker or
lockfile.

Override the base with `options(webterminal.port = 7681L)` or
`WEBTERMINAL_PORT=7681`.

## Load balancer considerations

If RStudio Server sits behind an ALB / nginx / Apache reverse proxy,
that proxy must:

1. **Allow WebSocket Upgrade** — pass `Upgrade: websocket` and
   `Connection: Upgrade` headers through to rserver. Most modern
   ALBs do this by default.

2. **Have a long idle timeout** — ttyd sends WebSocket ping frames
   every 20 seconds (`--ping-interval 20` in the launch scripts), but
   if the ALB's idle timeout is shorter than 20 seconds, the
   connection will be dropped between pings. Posit recommends at least
   60 seconds; for tmux sessions that may sit idle for hours, set the
   ALB idle timeout to 3600 seconds.

3. **Not strip `Sec-WebSocket-*` headers** — some WAFs strip these.
   Verify with:

```{bash}
curl -v \
  -H "Upgrade: websocket" \
  -H "Connection: Upgrade" \
  -H "Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==" \
  -H "Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13" \
  "https://<host>/p/<hex>/ws"
```

The response must be `101 Switching Protocols`. If you get `200` or
`400`, the proxy is intercepting the upgrade.

## Comparison with alternatives

| Approach | Installs new ports? | Outbound needed? | WebSocket? | Persist? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **webterminal (ttyd via /p/)** | No | No | Yes | Yes (tmux) |
| Raw SSH from laptop | Yes (port 22) | No | N/A | Yes (tmux/screen) |
| Tailscale / Cloudflare Tunnel | No | Yes (outbound) | N/A | Yes |
| AWS SSM Session Manager | No | Yes (outbound to AWS) | No | No |
| code-server via /p/ | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| RStudio built-in terminal | No | No | WS/RPC fallback | No |

webterminal occupies the "no new ports, no outbound, yes WebSocket,
yes persistence" cell that no other approach fills.
